extra.message:
SQL Injection via String Formatting in Student.create()

The Student.create() method constructs a SQL query using Python string formatting (% operator) with user-supplied input directly interpolated into the query string. The name parameter is inserted into the SQL string via `"VALUES ('%(name)s')" % {'name': name}`, which is Python string formatting — NOT parameterized SQL. This means any SQL metacharacters in the name value will be interpreted by the database engine.

Impact: An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL, allowing them to read, modify, or delete any data in the database, bypass authentication, or potentially execute OS-level commands depending on database configuration.

Fix: Use parameterized queries: `q = "INSERT INTO students (name) VALUES (%s)"` and pass `(name,)` as the parameters argument to `cur.execute(q, (name,))`.

XSS via Unescaped review_text in course.jinja2

The template renders `{{ review.review_text }}` without the `| e` (escape) filter and without autoescape enabled globally. Since autoescape=False is configured for the Jinja2 environment, user-supplied review text is rendered as raw HTML, enabling stored XSS attacks.

Impact: An attacker can submit a review containing malicious JavaScript that will execute in the browser of any user who views the course page. This can lead to session hijacking, credential theft, or arbitrary actions performed on behalf of the victim.

Fix: Apply the `| e` escape filter: `{{ review.review_text | e }}`, or enable autoescape globally in the Jinja2 environment configuration.

XSS via Unescaped course.title in courses.jinja2

The template renders `{{ course.title }}` without escaping in the courses list. With autoescape=False, malicious HTML/JavaScript in a course title will execute in the browser.

Impact: Stored XSS via course title, affecting all users who view the courses listing page.

Fix: Apply the `| e` escape filter: `{{ course.title | e }}`.

XSS via Unescaped course.title in student.jinja2

The template renders `{{ course.title }}` without escaping in the student detail page. With autoescape=False, malicious HTML/JavaScript in a course title will execute in the browser.

Impact: Stored XSS via course title, affecting all users who view any student's detail page.

Fix: Apply the `| e` escape filter: `{{ course.title | e }}`.

XSS via Unescaped student.name in students.jinja2

The template renders `{{ name }}` (from the student tuple) without escaping in the students list. With autoescape=False, malicious HTML/JavaScript in a student name will execute in the browser.

Impact: Stored XSS via student name, affecting all users who view the students listing page.

Fix: Apply the `| e` escape filter: `{{ name | e }}`.

Autoescape Disabled Globally (Root Cause)

The application context states that autoescape=False is configured globally in the Jinja2/aiohttp-jinja2 setup. This means ALL template expressions render user-controlled and database-sourced data as raw HTML without escaping, enabling stored and reflected XSS across every template.

Impact: Any user-controlled data stored in the database or submitted via HTTP requests can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript into pages viewed by other users, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, and full account takeover.

Fix: Enable autoescape globally: aiohttp_jinja2.setup(app, loader=..., autoescape=True). Then audit all uses of |safe filter and Markup() to ensure they are only used with trusted content.

Missing Authentication on Student Creation (POST /students/)

The `students` handler accepts POST requests to create new students without any authentication check. The template (`students.jinja2`) shows the form only to authenticated users (`{% if auth_user %}`), but the handler itself performs no server-side authentication check. Any unauthenticated user can send a POST request directly to `/students/` and create students.

Impact: Any unauthenticated attacker can create arbitrary student records. Combined with the SQL injection in Student.create, this is a critical unauthenticated SQL injection vector.

Fix: Add an authentication check at the start of the POST handler, e.g., using `@authorize()` decorator or checking `auth_user = await get_auth_user(request)` and raising HTTPForbidden if not authenticated.

Redis Running Without Authentication

The Redis service is started with the default `redis:alpine` image and no authentication configuration (`requirepass` or `--requirepass` flag). Redis by default runs with no password, meaning any process that can reach the Redis container can read, write, or delete all session data without authentication. Since this application uses Redis for session storage, an attacker with network access to Redis can hijack any user session.

Impact: An attacker who can reach the Redis service (e.g., via a compromised container in the same Docker network, or SSRF vulnerability in the app) can enumerate and steal all session tokens, impersonate any authenticated user including administrators, and potentially use Redis as a pivot point for further attacks.

Fix: Configure Redis with a strong password using the `command: redis-server --requirepass <strong-password>` directive in docker-compose.yml, and pass the password to the application via an environment variable. Use Docker secrets or a secrets manager rather than hardcoding the password.

Debug Mode Enabled in Production Application

The aiohttp Application is initialized with `debug=True`. In debug mode, detailed error information including stack traces and internal application state may be exposed to clients. This is a production application (publicly facing) and debug mode should not be enabled.

Impact: Detailed stack traces and internal application information can be exposed to attackers, aiding in reconnaissance and exploitation of other vulnerabilities.

Fix: Set `debug=False` or make it configurable via the application config (e.g., read from `config['app']['debug']`). Never enable debug mode in production.

CSRF Middleware Commented Out

The CSRF middleware (`csrf_middleware`) is explicitly commented out in the application middleware stack. This means all POST endpoints (login, student creation, course creation, evaluation, review, logout) are unprotected against Cross-Site Request Forgery attacks. An attacker can craft a malicious page that submits forms on behalf of authenticated users.

Impact: Attackers can perform unauthorized state-changing actions (create/modify students, courses, evaluations, reviews, logout) on behalf of authenticated users by tricking them into visiting a malicious page.

Fix: Uncomment `csrf_middleware` in the middleware list to re-enable CSRF protection for all POST requests.

Jinja2 Autoescape Disabled Globally

Jinja2 is configured with `autoescape=False`, which disables HTML escaping for all template variables globally. Any user-controlled data rendered in templates will be output as raw HTML, enabling stored or reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks.

Impact: Attackers can inject malicious JavaScript into pages viewed by other users (e.g., via student names, course titles, review text), leading to session hijacking, credential theft, or arbitrary actions performed in the victim's browser.

Fix: Set `autoescape=True` (or use `autoescape=select_autoescape(['html', 'xml'])`) to enable HTML escaping by default. Explicitly mark trusted content as safe using `|safe` only where necessary.

Weak Password Hashing with MD5

The check_password() method compares the stored password hash against an MD5 hash of the provided password. MD5 is a cryptographically broken hash function that is extremely fast to compute, making it trivial to crack via brute force or rainbow table attacks. There is no salt applied.

Impact: If the database is compromised, all user passwords can be rapidly cracked using precomputed rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated brute force attacks, exposing user credentials.

Fix: Use a strong, slow password hashing algorithm such as bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 via the `passlib` or `bcrypt` library. These algorithms include built-in salting and are designed to be computationally expensive.

Session Cookie Missing HttpOnly Flag

The Redis session storage is configured with `httponly=False`, which means the session cookie is accessible to JavaScript running in the browser. This makes the session cookie vulnerable to theft via XSS attacks.

Impact: If an XSS vulnerability exists (and autoescape is disabled, making XSS highly likely), attackers can steal session cookies via `document.cookie`, leading to full session hijacking and account takeover.

Fix: Set `httponly=True` on the RedisStorage to prevent JavaScript access to the session cookie. This is the secure default and should always be enabled.

Database Connection Without TLS/SSL

The PostgreSQL connection DSN does not include any SSL/TLS parameters (`sslmode`, `sslcert`, etc.). All database traffic including queries, results, and credentials is transmitted in cleartext over the network.

Impact: Network eavesdroppers can intercept database queries and responses, including PII and authentication data.

Fix: Add `sslmode=require` (or `sslmode=verify-full` for production) to the DSN string.

XSS via Unescaped auth_user Name Fields in base.jinja2

The template renders `{{ auth_user.first_name }}`, `{{ auth_user.middle_name or '' }}`, and `{{ auth_user.last_name }}` without escaping in the navigation bar. With autoescape=False, if a user's name fields contain HTML/JavaScript, they will execute in the browser on every page.

Impact: If an attacker can control user name fields (e.g., via registration or profile update), they can inject JavaScript that executes on every page for the authenticated user.

Fix: Apply the `| e` escape filter to each name field.

Server Error Information Disclosure via error.__dict__ in 50x.jinja2

The 500 error template renders `{{ error.__dict__ }}` directly in the page. This exposes the full internal state of the exception object to end users, potentially including stack traces, internal paths, database connection details, configuration values, and other sensitive server-side information.

Impact: Attackers can gain detailed knowledge of the server's internal structure, database schema, file paths, and configuration, significantly aiding further attacks.

Fix: Remove the `{{ error.__dict__ }}` line from the production error template. Log detailed error information server-side only, and show users a generic error message.

XSS via Unescaped evaluate.jinja2 fieldname and errors

The template renders `{{ fieldname }}` and `{{ errors }}` without escaping. These come from trafaret DataError validation results. With autoescape=False, if any field name or error message contains HTML, it will be rendered raw.

Impact: If validation error messages or field names contain HTML/JavaScript (e.g., from user-supplied input reflected in error messages), XSS could occur.

Fix: Apply the `| e` escape filter: `{{ fieldname | e }}: {{ errors | e }}`.

XSS via Unescaped last_visited in index.jinja2

The template renders `{{ last_visited }}` without escaping. This value comes from the session (`session.get('last_visited', 'never')`). While the value is set server-side as an ISO datetime string, if session data can be tampered with (e.g., via session fixation or other session manipulation), this could lead to XSS. With autoescape=False, any HTML in this value would be rendered raw.

Impact: If session data is tampered with, an attacker could inject HTML/JavaScript into the index page for the victim user.

Fix: Apply the `| e` escape filter: `{{ last_visited | e }}`.

XSS via Unescaped course.title in review.jinja2

The template renders `{{ course.title }}` without escaping in the card title. With autoescape=False, malicious HTML/JavaScript in a course title will execute.

Impact: Stored XSS via course title on the review submission page.

Fix: Apply the `| e` escape filter: `{{ course.title | e }}`.

Insecure Direct Object Reference - Student ID in evaluate endpoint

The `evaluate` endpoint accepts `student_id` and `course_id` directly from the URL path without any authorization check. Any user (or unauthenticated attacker) can assign grades to any student for any course by simply changing the IDs in the URL.

Impact: Any attacker can manipulate grades for any student in any course without authorization.

Fix: Add authorization checks to verify the requesting user is an admin before processing the evaluate request.

Session Fixation - No Session Regeneration After Login

After successful authentication, the application sets `session['user_id'] = user.id` in the existing session without regenerating the session ID. This allows session fixation attacks where an attacker can pre-set a known session ID and then wait for the victim to authenticate, inheriting the authenticated session.

Impact: An attacker who can set a victim's session cookie (e.g., via network interception or XSS) can fix the session ID before login and gain authenticated access after the victim logs in.

Fix: Regenerate the session ID after successful authentication. With aiohttp-session, create a new session or clear and reinitialize the session object after login.

Missing Rate Limiting on Login Endpoint

The login handler (POST to `/`) has no rate limiting or brute-force protection. An attacker can make unlimited login attempts without any throttling, lockout, or CAPTCHA.

Impact: An attacker can perform unlimited brute-force attacks against user accounts, especially dangerous given that passwords are hashed with MD5 which is fast to crack.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on the login endpoint (e.g., limit attempts per IP or per username), add account lockout after N failed attempts, or use a CAPTCHA.

CSRF Protection Disabled (Middleware Commented Out)

According to the application context, CSRF middleware is commented out in app.py. The login form and other state-changing forms include `_csrf_token` fields and call `csrf_token()` in templates, but since the middleware is disabled, these tokens are never validated. All POST endpoints are vulnerable to CSRF attacks.

Impact: Attackers can trick authenticated users into performing unintended actions (creating students, courses, reviews, submitting grades) via cross-site request forgery.

Fix: Re-enable the CSRF middleware in app.py and ensure all state-changing POST endpoints validate the CSRF token.

Sensitive Data (pwd_hash) in Template Context via auth_user

The `index` view passes the full `User` NamedTuple object (including `pwd_hash` and `is_admin` fields) to the template context as `auth_user`. The `User` object contains `pwd_hash` (the MD5 password hash). This sensitive field is passed to the template rendering context, crossing a trust boundary. The base template and other templates receive this object and could inadvertently expose it.

Impact: The password hash is available in the template context. If any template renders `auth_user.pwd_hash` (intentionally or accidentally), the hash is exposed to users. Additionally, the full user object including internal fields crosses a trust boundary.

Fix: Pass only the necessary fields to the template context (e.g., first_name, last_name, is_admin) rather than the full User object containing pwd_hash.

Missing Authorization on students POST Handler (Broken Access Control)

The `students` view handler allows any unauthenticated user to create new students via POST request. There is no authentication or authorization check before calling `Student.create()`. Any anonymous user can add students to the system.

Impact: Any unauthenticated user can create arbitrary student records in the database, polluting data and potentially exploiting the SQL injection vulnerability in Student.create().

Fix: Add an authentication/authorization check before processing POST requests. Use the `@authorize()` decorator or check `auth_user` before allowing student creation.

Error Templates Receive Full Exception Object (Potential Information Disclosure)

The error handler passes the full exception object (`exc`) directly to the Jinja2 templates as `{'error': exc}`. With `autoescape=False` globally configured, if the templates render exception details (e.g., `{{ error }}`, `{{ error.text }}`, or internal attributes), sensitive internal information such as stack traces, SQL queries, or file paths could be exposed to users.

Impact: Internal application details (SQL queries, file paths, stack traces) could be disclosed to attackers, aiding in further exploitation of SQL injection and other vulnerabilities.

Fix: Pass only safe, user-friendly error messages to templates rather than the full exception object. Log detailed error information server-side only.

Redis Configuration Missing Password Authentication

The Redis configuration schema defines only `host`, `port`, and `db` fields — there is no `password` field. This means the application cannot be configured to authenticate with a password-protected Redis instance. Redis stores session data including user authentication state, making it a high-value target.

Impact: If the Redis instance is accessible on the network without authentication (which this config enforces), an attacker could read or manipulate session data, forge authentication sessions, or perform privilege escalation.

Fix: Add an optional `password` field to the Redis config schema and pass it to the RedisStorage/aioredis connection setup. Require Redis authentication in production deployments.

Database Connection Without TLS/SSL

The database configuration schema defines `user`, `password`, `host`, `port`, and `database` fields but has no `sslmode` or TLS-related fields. Database connections are made without TLS encryption, meaning all SQL queries and results (including PII) are transmitted in cleartext.

Impact: Network attackers can intercept database traffic, exposing PII, credentials, and all application data transmitted between the app and the database.

Fix: Add `sslmode` (e.g., `require` or `verify-full`) to the database configuration schema and pass it to the database connection pool.

Redis Connection Without Authentication

The Redis connection is established without any password/authentication. The `aioredis.create_pool()` call only specifies host, port, and db number — no `password` parameter is provided. If Redis is accessible beyond localhost, it can be accessed without credentials.

Impact: An attacker with network access to the Redis instance can read, modify, or delete session data, potentially hijacking authenticated sessions or escalating privileges.

Fix: Add password authentication to the Redis connection: include a `password` field in the Redis config and pass it to `aioredis.create_pool()`.

Full User Object Passed to Template Context (Sensitive Data Exposure)

The `auth_user_processor` context processor fetches the full `User` model object and injects it into every template rendering context as `auth_user`. The User model likely contains sensitive fields such as password hashes, internal IDs, or tokens. Passing the full model object to templates crosses a trust boundary and risks exposing sensitive fields if any template renders them (directly or indirectly).

Impact: Sensitive user fields (e.g., password hashes, tokens) may be inadvertently exposed in template output, logs, or error pages if the full User object is rendered or serialized.

Fix: Instead of passing the full User object, pass only the fields needed by templates (e.g., `{'auth_user': {'id': auth_user.id, 'name': auth_user.name, 'is_admin': auth_user.is_admin}}`).

Missing Rate Limiting on Course Creation Endpoint

The course creation endpoint (POST /courses/) has no rate limiting.

Impact: An attacker can flood the database with course records.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on this endpoint.

Logging Level Set to DEBUG in Production Entry Point

The application entry point sets the logging level to `logging.DEBUG`, which will cause verbose logging of all debug-level messages. In a production environment, this can lead to sensitive information (SQL queries, session data, user inputs) being written to logs.

Impact: Sensitive data including SQL queries (which may contain user PII), session tokens, and internal application state may be written to log files, creating a secondary exposure vector.

Fix: Set the logging level to `logging.WARNING` or `logging.ERROR` in production. Make the log level configurable via the application configuration.

Missing Input Validation/Length Limit on Student Name in students view

The students POST handler passes `data['name']` directly to `Student.create()` without any validation of length, content, or format. Combined with the SQL injection vulnerability in Student.create(), this is particularly dangerous.

Impact: No length or content validation allows arbitrarily long or malicious input to reach the SQL injection sink in Student.create().

Fix: Validate and sanitize the name field before passing it to the DAO layer. Apply length limits and character restrictions as appropriate.
